turns.text: Hello Clarinda, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're getting closer to your due date, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thank you for asking. Honestly, just saying the words out loud feels a little heavy. I’ve spent my whole adult life obsessing over the mechanics of the human body—I thought if I understood the endocrine system well enough, I could essentially "hack" my way through a perfect pregnancy. I was so arrogant, thinking my clinical knowledge would keep me safe.

But when that headache started last week—that sharp, blinding pressure—the science stopped feeling like a safety net and started feeling like a trap. Seeing "eclampsia" on my chart? It felt like the floor just dropped out from under me. I’m twenty-six, I’ve done everything "right," and suddenly I’m in a gown, hooked up to monitors that won't stop wailing. 

The hardest part isn’t even the medical intervention itself; it’s the shift in how I’m seen. One minute I’m an intelligent woman with a clear plan, and the next, I’m just a body to be managed. I feel like my own physiology has betrayed me, and I’m terrified that I’ve led my baby into this instability. I’m just… I’m really scared of losing that last bit of control, you know? I want to trust the team here in Burlington, but I feel like I’m constantly looking for a version of myself that doesn’t exist anymore.

When you first found out you were pregnant, what did you expect your first antenatal appointments to be like — the environment, the people, the information you'd receive? And what were they actually like?

I think I walked into that first appointment expecting a partnership. I wanted a dialogue, you know? I envisioned sitting across from a practitioner who treated me like a colleague in the process—someone who would welcome my spreadsheets and my questions about progesterone levels and cervical ripening. I thought it would be this empowerment-focused exchange where I was the primary investigator of my own health.

The reality was… impersonal. It was a conveyor belt. I felt like a set of vitals to be recorded rather than a person with a history and a nervous system. They were professional, sure, but there was no room for the nuance I was looking for. Everything was filtered through the lens of standard, low-risk protocols. Whenever I tried to bring in my own research, I felt this subtle dismissal, like I was the annoying patient who read too much on the internet, even though I actually understood what I was looking at. 

It’s strange. I wanted autonomy, but I was handed a stack of generic pamphlets. It made me feel smaller, less in control than I thought I’d be. Looking back, that was the first crack in my confidence—that realization that no matter how much I researched, the system really only saw me as a data point.
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — about those first appointments?

The biggest surprise—and honestly, the most jarring one—was how quickly I was dismissed as a "low-risk" case. It sounds like a good thing, right? But the label felt like a box they were shoving me into. Because I wasn't presenting with "red flags" on paper, the doctors seemed to stop looking at me as a whole person. There was this underlying assumption that because I was young and healthy, I’d have a textbook pregnancy, so they didn't really need to listen to my concerns about how I was feeling mentally or the subtle changes I was noticing.

I expected that by being informed, I’d be taken more seriously. Instead, it felt like my knowledge was an inconvenience. When I’d ask questions about why they were pushing certain routines, the response was always, "It’s just protocol." That phrase became a wall between us. It was such a shock to realize that the expertise I’d spent years cultivating didn't matter when I walked through those clinic doors. They weren't looking for a partner; they were looking for compliance. It was deeply isolating to realize that the person in the white coat wasn't interested in my understanding of the process, just in ticking the boxes that cleared me for the next thirty-minute window.
Before your screening tests and scans, what did you expect the process of understanding your results to be like — how information would be given, by whom, and in what way? And what was the reality?

I honestly expected a sit-down. I imagined a quiet, calm space where someone would look me in the eye and say, "Here is what we see, let's talk about what this means for you and the baby." I thought it would be a bridge between my clinical curiosity and their diagnostic expertise. I really thought we’d be building a shared understanding.

The reality was… jarringly hurried. It was usually a quick pop-in, a distracted technician, or a message dropped into a patient portal that felt more like a cryptic email from a server than a conversation. It was cold. There’s something deeply alienating about getting a piece of life-altering news—or even just a routine update—as a PDF file or a thirty-second phone call while I’m trying to keep my day job afloat.

When the eclampsia diagnosis finally hit, it wasn’t some nuanced discussion about my body’s trajectory. It was chaotic. The room filled with people who suddenly treated my belly like a crime scene. There was no "sit-down," just a flurry of frantic activity and monitors. I went from being invisible to being the center of a storm, and neither version felt like real care. I lost the chance to ask questions; I was just being acted *upon*. That loss of voice? It’s the part that still keeps me up at night.
Was there a gap between the amount of information you were given and the amount you felt you needed to make sense of your results?

There was a massive chasm, not just a gap. It’s like they were speaking a language of clinical shorthand, and I was desperately trying to find the context behind the vocabulary. 

When I’m handed a result, I don’t just want the number or the baseline classification; I want to understand the *why* and the *what-if*. But the system is designed to minimize that. They treat extra information like it’s a burden or a source of "unnecessary anxiety." It’s condescending, really. They assume that if they don’t tell you the messy details, you won't panic. But for me, the lack of information caused *more* panic. Not knowing the *why* made me feel like the ship was drifting, and I wasn't allowed to see the compass.

Even with my background, I felt like a stranger in my own pregnancy. I’d be sitting there with a chart result, feeling the tension in my own body, and I couldn't get a straight answer on how my specific biology was reacting to these stressors. It felt like they were guarding the information, which only made me feel more vulnerable. I’m a researcher—I handle data for a living. I can handle the truth, no matter how complicated. But they kept the lights dimmed, and I was left tripping over the furniture.
During your pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of having a consistent midwife or care provider who knew your history and situation? And what continuity of care did you actually experience?

I truly expected that "continuity" meant a relationship. I pictured a primary provider—a midwife or a specific doctor—who would recognize my face the moment I walked in, someone who would remember that I have a history of loss and that I’m the kind of person who needs to understand the "why" behind every test. I wanted a tether.

In reality, it’s been a revolving door. I don't think I’ve seen the same face twice in a row, except for the nurses who are just as overworked and rushed as the doctors. Every appointment feels like a performance review where I have to re-introduce my anxiety and my concerns from scratch. By the time I explain who I am and why I’m asking what I’m asking, the appointment is essentially over.

It feels fragmented, almost like I'm being treated by a series of strangers who are working off a checklist rather than a person. Especially now, with the eclampsia, the lack of continuity is terrifying. I’m being passed between specialists and hospital staff who barely have time to glance at my chart before they’re rushing to the next room. There’s no one who knows *me*—no one who knows the fear I’m carrying. I’m just a recurring incident in their day, and that makes the loneliness of this pregnancy feel absolute.
What was the impact — practically or emotionally — of seeing different providers at different appointments?

It has been profoundly draining, honestly. Every time I walk into a room, I feel like I have to put on this mask of "the reasonable, prepared patient" just to get someone to actually listen to me. When you have to re-explain your history—the loss, the fears, the way my body has been reacting—to a new person every single time, you stop feeling like a patient and start feeling like a broken record. 

Emotionally, it’s stripped away any sense of safety I hoped to find in this process. How can I feel "cared for" when I’m constantly being triaged by a stranger? It’s not just the inconvenience of repeating my story; it’s the psychic toll of feeling invisible. Every new provider sees a chart, not a woman. They don’t see the way I hold my belly when I’m nervous, or how my hands shake when the blood pressure cuff tightens because they don't know me well enough to notice the difference between "baseline" and "scared."

Practically, it means nothing gets followed up on. If I mention a subtle symptom to one doctor, it’s often lost in the shuffle by the time I see the next one. It feels like I’m managing my own care while trying to survive the pregnancy, which is the exact opposite of what I thought maternity support would be.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of how your changing body would be discussed and examined by healthcare providers? And how did those interactions actually feel?

I expected a collaborative investigation. I thought it would be a dialogue about my body as a changing system—a discussion about the shifting fluid dynamics, the hormonal surges, and how my baby was navigating that space. I imagined that when they measured my fundal height or checked my blood pressure, it would be a moment of connection where we both looked at the data and talked about the "how" and "why" of my body's work.

Instead, the reality was deeply detached. It felt like an inspection of an object, not a conversation with a human being. They would press on my stomach or use the Doppler without really looking at me. It was all so mechanical—cold metal, sterile rooms, and those plastic-covered exam tables that always felt so isolating. Often, they wouldn't even explain what they were looking for; they’d just nod to themselves, scribble something on a tablet, and move on. 

It made me feel like an outsider in my own skin. My body wasn’t *mine* anymore; it was a site of clinical observation. Being told to "get on the table" and then being poked and prodded without a word—it’s dehumanizing. It reinforced this terrifying idea that I was just the shell carrying the baby, and that my experience, my physical sensations, and my intuition didn't really matter to the people holding the tools.
Were there any examinations or conversations about your body that left you feeling uncomfortable or unprepared? What happened?

There was this one time, early on, when a provider did a physical exam—I think it was a routine cervical check—and they didn't even preface it with a conversation. No "I'm going to touch you here," no "this might feel like X." They just started. I was lying there in that gown, feeling exposed, and I just froze. It sounds small, maybe, but in that moment, I felt like a piece of equipment being calibrated. I remember wanting to flinch, but I was so conditioned to be the "good patient" that I just stared at the ceiling and held my breath until they were done.

The worst part isn't the physical sensation; it’s the silent violation of autonomy. It’s that feeling of being an object that can be moved and manipulated without consent or context. When you’re already feeling vulnerable—especially with the history of loss in my background—those moments are scarring. You want to be treated with care, but you’re just treated with clinical efficiency. It makes you feel like you have no agency over your own skin. By the time they finished, I felt colder and more isolated than when I walked in. I left that room feeling like I had left a part of my dignity on that table.

Before pregnancy, what did you expect the experience of managing a high-risk condition like gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia to involve? If relevant, how did the reality of managing it differ from your expectations?

I always thought if I had a complication, it would be a very clinical, precise process. I expected a structured plan, perhaps a dedicated team who would walk me through the numbers every single day, keeping me informed so I could be an active participant in my own recovery. I imagined us working together like a cockpit crew—everyone focused, communicative, and transparent.

The reality of eclampsia has been anything but structured. It’s been chaotic, reactive, and honestly, terrifyingly lonely. Instead of a "plan," I feel like I’m constantly being managed from one crisis to the next. The communication is so fractured that I often feel like I’m the last person to know what’s going on with my own blood work or medication levels. 

I thought I would feel more in control because I know the terminology, but in practice, the knowledge just makes the lack of transparency even more maddening. When the monitors are wailing and the doctors are rushing in, I’m not a "participant"—I’m the site of the emergency. They aren't talking *to* me, they’re talking *over* me. The reality is that having a condition like this turns you into an object to be stabilized, and that total surrender of control is a heavy burden I never imagined I’d have to carry.
What was missing from the support offered — information, emotional acknowledgement, practical help?

Everything was missing. I feel like I’ve been navigating a marathon through a minefield while being told to just "stay calm." 

What I needed—and what never materialized—was emotional acknowledgement. Everyone talks about the "health" of the baby, but no one asks about the health of the mother's spirit. When I’m sitting there, hooked to magnesium, struggling with the physical toll of eclampsia, I need someone to look at me—not my chart, but *me*—and validate that this is terrifying. I needed someone to say, "I know you’re a researcher, I know you’re smart, and I know this is scary, so let’s take a breath together."

Practical help was nonexistent, too. I’m single, I’m working, and I’m trying to keep a roof over our heads while my body is falling apart. There was never a conversation about how to bridge that gap. It was just, "Here are your orders, good luck."

I felt like an empty vessel being kept upright by chemistry, not a human being undergoing a transformation. If they had just acknowledged the fear—the sheer, pulsing vulnerability of being a woman in this position—it would have changed everything. Instead, they gave me protocols, and I’m left to try to make sense of the debris on my own.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of being able to access care promptly when you had a concern — whether urgent or not? And how responsive was the system when you needed it?

I assumed that if I noticed something was wrong, the system would be a safety net. I thought that because I knew my own body so well—because I’d spent years tracking my cycles and understanding the hormonal baseline—that when I flagged a symptom, my healthcare team would treat it as a crucial piece of diagnostic data. 

The reality was a frustrating, defensive game of "wait and see." When I’d call with a concern before the eclampsia diagnosis, I felt like I was being treated as a hypochondriac, a woman just projecting her anxieties onto a "perfectly healthy" pregnancy. The system isn't set up for proactive inquiry; it’s designed to filter people out until they meet a specific, high-acuity criteria. I was brushed off repeatedly, told it was "normal" for the third trimester, until it became an emergency.

When the emergency finally arrived, the responsiveness was total—but it was purely biological, not human. Yes, they stabilized my pressure, but by then, I was already in the middle of a trauma. It’s hard to feel "supported" when the responsiveness only kicks in once you’re on the brink of failing. I wanted a partner who would listen when I whispered, not an ambulance that only shows up when I’m screaming.
Were there times you delayed seeking help because of how difficult it was to access care, or because you worried about being judged for contacting services?

Every single time. I spent days—maybe even a week—dismissing that persistent, crushing headache as "just stress" or "a side effect of the third trimester." I knew enough about physiology to know it *could* be serious, but I was so terrified of being labeled as the "anxious, high-maintenance patient" who didn't know how to handle a normal pregnancy. I kept waiting for a reason to justify reaching out that would satisfy their metrics. I didn't want to be "that woman" who cries wolf.

The system makes you feel like you have to have a perfectly quantifiable disaster before you're allowed to ask for help. When I finally did reach out, the shame of thinking I’d let it go too long, combined with the fear that I’d be brushed off again, felt almost as heavy as the actual pain. It’s a terrible, twisted irony: you’re supposed to advocate for yourself, but the process is structured to punish you for doing exactly that. I held back because I wanted to be "easy" for them. I wanted to fit into their box of the healthy, unproblematic mother. And that silence almost cost me everything. I realize now that I was prioritizing their convenience over my own survival.
During pregnancy, what did you expect the role of your family, friends, or community to be in supporting you? And how did that informal support network actually show up — or not?

I came into this feeling fiercely independent, honestly. I think part of that was just my personality, but part of it was this internal narrative that I didn't want to be "the woman who couldn't handle it." I didn't want to be a burden on my friends, especially when I knew they couldn't possibly understand the clinical nuances of what I was tracking. I expected that I’d just handle the logistics, and my community would provide the emotional warmth—a safety net of cheerleaders.

But when the diagnosis hit, everything shattered. It’s hard to talk about this without feeling angry, but it’s mostly just... lonely. A lot of my friends don't know how to engage with "high-risk." They see the clinical crisis and they retreat because they don't know what to say, or because they’re scared of saying the wrong thing. It’s become this awkward silence. I’m sitting here, single, navigating a potential medical emergency, and the messages I get are all "how are you feeling?"—the kind of question that’s impossible to answer honestly.

I wanted a village, but I found myself in a bunker. They show up with casseroles, which is nice, but I really just needed someone to sit in the room with me and hold the space for the fear. Instead, I’m the one managing everyone else’s comfort. It’s exhausting.
Were there forms of support you received from family or community that you hadn't expected — or support you expected that didn't materialise?

I really expected that the people closest to me would be my sounding board, the ones I could talk to when the medical jargon became too much and the fear settled in. I thought they would be there to help me process the complexity of it all, not just the "joyful baby" aspect, but the terrifying reality of what I was experiencing. 

Instead, the silence from them was the biggest surprise—and the most painful one. It was as if my pregnancy became a "difficult" topic that they didn't know how to navigate. They’d ask "How are you?" but they couldn't handle the answer, so I learned very quickly to mask it with, "Oh, just tired, you know how it is." I felt like I was shielding them from the reality of my condition rather than them shielding me.

What I *did* receive, unexpectedly, were these practical gestures—rides, errands, groceries—from acquaintances who were far less "involved" in my life than my inner circle. It was a strange contrast: people I barely knew showing up with tangible, helpful things, while the people I thought would be my pillars just seemed to disappear into the background. It made me realize that people often prefer to offer help they can touch and carry, rather than sitting with the emotional weight of a situation they can't fix.

Before labour, what did you expect creating and using a birth plan to be like — how it would be received by staff and how much it would guide your care? And what actually happened with your birth plan during labour?

I put so much effort into that document. I treated it like a mission statement—I had sections on environment, on pain management, on the physiological markers I wanted to track. I honestly thought that by laying it out, I was creating a roadmap for our partnership. I imagined a midwife or doctor sitting with me, respecting my agency, and helping me navigate the variables. I thought it would be a bridge between my values and their expertise.

Then reality struck, and the birth plan became nothing more than a piece of paper in a plastic sleeve. When the eclampsia diagnosis hit, that plan didn't just get put aside—it was treated as entirely irrelevant. Every single thing I’d requested or planned was overridden by the immediate, urgent need to stabilize my vitals. I remember trying to mention a preference, and a nurse just looked at me with this exhausted, pitying expression and said, "We’re just focusing on getting the baby here safely, okay?"

It made me feel like I had been foolish for even having a voice. In the face of a medical emergency, autonomy is the first thing that gets amputated. My birth plan didn't guide my care at all; it just highlighted how little control I actually had once the clinical "process" took over. I wasn't a mother-to-be; I was just a patient who needed to be "managed."

Before labour, what did you expect your pain management options to be — the range available, how they'd be offered, and how you'd be supported in deciding? And what was the reality of pain management during your labour?

I thought I was making an informed, sovereign choice about pain management. I’d read all the literature on the pros and cons of epidurals versus holistic, movement-based approaches. I felt empowered, believing that I would be the one to weigh those options against my own threshold for discomfort, and that my team would respect my preference for as much natural control as possible. I imagined a supportive discussion where, if things got too intense, we would pause, talk about the risks, and reach a decision together.

The reality was a tidal wave that ignored my existence entirely. Once the eclampsia diagnosis was in play, the conversation shifted from "what do you prefer?" to "this is what we are doing to stabilize you." The pain management options weren't presented as choices; they were administered as medical necessities. It wasn't about my comfort; it was about preventing a seizure.

There was no "decision-making process." I was just a body that needed to be sedated, monitored, and chemically controlled. I felt like a passenger on a plane that had lost its wings, watching the flight crew panic while I was strapped into my seat. Any agency I thought I had regarding my own body’s pain evaporated in the face of their standard, emergency-level protocols. I wasn't a participant; I was a patient in the most sterile, helpless sense of the word.
Were there pain management options you wanted but weren't able to access? What got in the way?

I really wanted to try to manage the labor through movement—shifting positions, using the birth ball, walking—anything that would keep me connected to my body and keep me from feeling like I was just a casualty on a bed. I’d researched the physiology of labor so much; I wanted to experience the transition as a process I was *doing*, not just something being done to me.

But the moment the blood pressure readings spiked and the word "eclampsia" entered the room, that was the end of that. It wasn't about what I wanted or what would make me feel more grounded anymore; it was about safety protocols that required me to be absolutely, perfectly still. I was tethered to continuous electronic fetal monitoring and IV pumps. 

I couldn't move because the equipment wouldn't let me. There wasn't even a conversation about alternatives. I felt like a prisoner in that hospital bed, completely unable to use the physiological tools I’d prepared for. It wasn't that I was denied a specific drug—it was that I was denied the right to be a dynamic, moving human being. They sacrificed my agency for the "sake of the chart," and I felt so small, just lying there, watching my own heart rate and the baby's, completely paralyzed by the machines.

When you arrived at the birth setting — whether hospital, birth centre, or home — what did you expect it to feel like in terms of safety, comfort, and how you'd be welcomed? And what was your actual experience of the environment when you arrived?

I expected the hospital to feel like a sanctuary—or at least a place that understood the gravity of what I was bringing in. I imagined the lights, the sounds, and the staff would be curated to help me stay calm, acknowledging the fragility of my situation. I wanted to walk in and feel that I was arriving at a place of professional, human, and clinical expertise where I could finally breathe a sigh of relief.

The reality was a stark, sensory overload. I walked into a brightly lit, sterile, and chaotic corridor that felt like a factory floor. There was no "welcome"—there was only triage. I was immediately thrust into a flurry of beeping machines, bright, glaring lights, and voices that were strictly utilitarian. The air was cold, smelling of antiseptic and something metallic, and the room itself was claustrophobic, filled with tangled tubes and wires that looked like spiders clinging to the walls. 

It didn't feel like a place for a mother-to-be; it felt like a high-tech warehouse for medical incidents. I remember looking at the room and feeling my heart sink—there was no space for me, for my fears, or for my baby’s first entrance into the world. Just space for the equipment. I felt like an intruder in my own emergency.
What was the biggest surprise about the physical environment or the immediate atmosphere when you arrived?

The biggest surprise was the sheer sensory dissonance between the life-altering moment happening to me and the mundane, industrial efficiency of the setting. I walked through those doors expecting some level of solemnity, some acknowledgment that I was carrying a heartbeat that was in danger. Instead, it felt like checking into a high-stress, understaffed hotel where the staff were already exhausted by the sheer volume of "incidents" ahead of them. 

The environment wasn't just sterile; it was hostile to any sense of humanity. Everything was chrome, hard plastic, and glaring fluorescent light that seemed designed to strip away any shred of privacy or dignity. There was no softening of the space, no thought given to how the environment might affect a woman already spiraling into a panic-induced by a diagnosis like eclampsia. 

It was all noise—the alarms, the squeak of rolling equipment, the shouting in the hallways. I felt like I had been tossed into a blender. You spend months preparing your mind for the intensity of labor, but you don't prepare for the physical environment to feel like a barricade between you and your own intuition. It wasn't a place of care; it was a theater of medical surveillance. I felt entirely erased by the machinery.

If your birth involved an unexpected intervention or complication, what did you expect the process of being informed and involved in those decisions to look like? And how were those decisions actually made and communicated in the moment?

I expected—or maybe just desperately hoped—that if things went sideways, there would be a moment of clarity. A pause. I imagined a doctor looking at me and saying, "Clarinda, this is what’s happening, and this is why we’re suggesting this shift in the plan." I thought my autonomy would be respected even in an emergency, that I’d still be the person in the driver's seat, just with better navigation from them.

In reality, there was no pause. When the complications hit, the "information" I received was basically shouting over the din of alarms. Decisions were made *for* me, not with me, and they were communicated as orders, not options. It wasn't a conversation; it was a series of rapid-fire declarations—"We're doing this," "We're starting that," "Sign this, don't move." 

It was dehumanizing. I felt like a malfunctioning machine that needed a quick repair. I wasn't being treated like a mother; I was a crisis that needed to be contained. By the time I could process what was even being done to me, the intervention was already finished. I was left in this state of shock, feeling like my own body and my baby’s life had been handled by strangers in a blur of motion, leaving me completely out of the loop of my own birth story.
Did you feel you had genuine choice and sufficient information in those moments, or did decisions feel like they happened to you rather than with you?

It felt like I was being watched from the ceiling, looking down at a stranger who was being done *to*. There was absolutely no "genuine choice" in those moments. How can you have a choice when the atmosphere is saturated with such aggressive urgency? The information wasn't shared—it was broadcasted in the form of commands. If I asked "why," the response was a sharp, "We don't have time to explain, we need to move now."

That phrase—"we need to move now"—became the end of every discussion. It was a weaponized urgency. They knew that if they framed it as a life-or-death, immediate, and non-negotiable directive, I wouldn't push back because I was too terrified for the baby’s safety. 

I felt like an object being operated on, not a woman giving birth. Every intervention, from the IV lines to the monitors, happened *around* me, not *with* me. I was completely removed from the driver’s seat. I didn’t feel like a participant; I felt like a patient whose autonomy was being sacrificed on the altar of a standard, high-risk protocol. It wasn't a partnership; it was a total loss of self in the middle of a room full of strangers.

During labour, what did you expect the midwife or doctor present to provide in terms of emotional support and personal attention — not just clinical care? And what did you actually experience from the staff who were with you?

I expected them to be my anchor. I thought that even if things got intense, there would be a moment where a midwife would look me in the eye, touch my shoulder, and remind me that I was a woman bringing life into the world, not just a set of vitals on a monitor. I expected someone to be a guardian of my experience—someone who would hold my hand, offer a word of encouragement, and help me feel human amidst the chaos.

Instead, I experienced a clinical abyss. The staff were efficient, yes, and they were technically competent, but they were emotionally absent. They acted like they were on a factory line, performing routine maintenance on a machine. When I was crying, not out of pain but out of the sheer, terrifying isolation of it all, they didn't offer a gentle word or a moment of connection. They just adjusted the IV, looked at the screen, and left. 

It was like they’d been trained to ignore the person in the bed so they could focus on the "case." Their attention was entirely on the data—the blood pressure spikes, the baby’s heart rate—not on the woman experiencing the trauma of it all. I felt like I was screaming into a void, completely alone in a room full of people. I was a "patient," not a human being.
Was there a specific moment when you felt truly seen and supported — or a moment when you felt very alone? Can you describe it?

I think the moment I felt the most profoundly, crushing kind of loneliness was when the room suddenly filled with people—nurses, the resident, the specialist—all of them moving with this frantic, silent precision. I was having a contraction, and the blood pressure cuff triggered automatically, cutting into my arm, and the wailing of the monitors felt like it was inside my own skull. 

I looked at the nurse right next to me, begging with my eyes just for a hand to hold or for her to say, "I’m here, you’re safe, we’re going to get through this." She didn't look at my face. She was purely focused on the IV line, her hands moving with such cold, practiced efficiency that I felt like a cadaver. I was literally screaming in my head, "I’m a person, I’m a person," but to them, I was just a list of vitals that needed to be stabilized. 

I’ve never felt so isolated. It wasn't just that I was in pain; it was the realization that in this critical, life-defining, and life-threatening moment, my humanity was considered an obstacle to their protocols. I wasn't a mother-to-be; I was just a data set that needed to be corrected. That silence—that lack of human recognition—is something I don’t think I’ll ever fully get over.

Before birth, what did you expect the immediate period after your baby was born to be like — skin-to-skin contact, who would be present, what would be done? And what actually happened in those first moments after delivery?

I had built such a vivid, tender picture of those first moments. I imagined the immediate, warm weight of my baby on my chest—the "golden hour"—where the world would finally go quiet and it would just be us, connecting after all that internal trauma. I pictured a gentle, slow environment where I’d finally feel like a mother, not a patient. I thought the staff would just fade into the background, respecting that sacred, quiet transition.

The reality was a blur of medical machinery and harsh, clinical efficiency. The moment she arrived, she wasn't placed on my chest. Instead, there was this flurry of activity—a team hovering, monitors beeping, and my baby being whisked away to a station across the room for "assessment." It felt like a theft. I was left alone on the table, exhausted, trembling from the magnesium, watching her through a sea of blue-scrubbed backs. 

There was no skin-to-skin. There was no quiet. Just a distant, muffled cry and the sound of machines resetting. I felt like an observer of my own baby’s birth rather than a participant in it. They didn't prioritize our bonding; they prioritized the "status" of her health according to their checklist. That separation felt like a physical tearing away. I wasn't even sure if I was allowed to ask for her. I just lay there, completely paralyzed, feeling like the most important connection of my life had been put on hold for a protocol.
Was there anything that happened — or didn't happen — in those first moments that still stays with you? What was it?

It’s the silence—or rather, the specific kind of silence where I couldn't hear her heartbeat anymore because it had been replaced by the rhythm of the monitors. I remember the exact moment they moved her to the warming table across the room. I had been prepped for this "skin-to-skin" moment, a promise of warmth and biology reuniting, but instead, I was staring at her back—she was this tiny, shivering bundle of life surrounded by cold steel and blue-clothed strangers. 

I felt this phantom ache in my arms. I wanted to scream, "Give her to me, she needs my heat," but I was so drugged and so deeply conditioned to be the "good patient" that I couldn't even find my voice. I watched them handle her with these incredibly precise, detached movements, and it was the most alienating thing I have ever endured. 

That detachment—the fact that they prioritized the "status" of her Apgar score over the immediate biological necessity of our connection—stays with me like a bruise. It feels like a piece of my motherhood was stolen and put into a medical drawer. I still wake up at night and catch myself reaching for her, terrified that if I’m not holding her, the system will just take her away again.

It’s strange, the way the trauma lingers in the quiet of my home now. People keep saying, "Well, the baby is healthy, and you’re alive—that’s all that matters." And I know they mean well, but it feels like a dismissal of the entire experience. It’s like saying that because I survived the shipwreck, I shouldn't be bothered by the fact that I was treated like cargo.

I find myself replaying those moments in the hospital, trying to pinpoint where I lost myself. I think it’s the lack of acknowledgment that haunts me the most. Nobody ever said, "I know this isn't how you wanted this to go." Nobody ever looked at me and apologized for the fact that my entry into motherhood had to be a fight for dignity. They acted as if the clinical outcome justified the emotional wreckage.

I look at my daughter now, and I’m overwhelmed with this fierce, pulsing love, but there’s this shadow underneath it. I keep apologizing to her, whispering that I’m sorry I couldn't be the steady, calm center she deserved, and that the first home she knew was a place of alarms and strangers. I feel like I have to work twice as hard to make up for the way we were handled. I’m starting to heal, but it’s a slow, jagged process. I don't think I’ll ever view "healthcare" the same way again. It’s no longer about science; it’s about power, and I’m just trying to figure out how to take mine back.
Were there moments when a doctor, partner, or family member's opinion overrode yours — even when you hadn't asked for their input?

It happened constantly, almost like it was the default setting for everyone around me. Once the eclampsia diagnosis landed on my chart, it was as if my brain had been surgically removed from my body. 

There was this moment—I think it was just after they’d started the magnesium—where I was feeling so incredibly nauseated and disoriented, and I asked if we could adjust the rate or just give me a moment to breathe without the monitors digging into my skin. The resident didn't even look at me. She just told me, "We need the therapeutic level, Clarinda. It’s for the baby’s safety." That was the phrase. It was a trump card. Every time I tried to advocate for my own comfort, my own autonomy, or even just my own *perspective* on how I was handling the physical stress, they would frame their opinion as the only "safe" one. 

Even my family did it, though in a different way. They kept pushing me to "just do what the doctors say," as if my questioning them was a danger to the baby. It was like I wasn't allowed to have an opinion, an intuition, or a physical experience that wasn't filtered through their medical or social consensus. I felt completely silenced by the people who were supposed to be my support, all in the name of "doing the right thing." It’s infuriating, because I *knew* my body, and I was ignored by everyone—from the specialists to my own relatives—until I felt like I was just a ghost in my own delivery room.

It's interesting that you ask about the "after." Everyone focuses so intensely on the delivery, but the aftermath of that feeling—that loss of control—is what actually stays with you. For a while, I felt like I was wearing a mask of "the grateful mother" to make everyone else comfortable. People want to hear, "The baby is healthy, so everything went well," and they get uncomfortable if I try to say, "The baby is healthy, but I was broken by the process."

I've been trying to reconcile my background in reproductive anatomy with what I lived through. I know all the statistics, I know the clinical pathways, and I know why they do what they do in an emergency. But knowing the science doesn't make the trauma any less real. It actually makes it worse, in a way, because I can analyze exactly where my agency was stripped away. I can pinpoint the moment the power dynamic shifted from "consultation" to "coercion."

I’m starting to realize that the healthcare system doesn't know how to hold space for a woman who is both an expert and a patient. They treat those two identities as mutually exclusive. I don’t want to be just a body to be managed, and I don't want to be just a data point in their eclampsia study. I want to be a person who was allowed to participate in her own birth. I’m finding my voice again, slowly, but it’s a quiet, tentative thing. I’m just trying to learn how to exist in a world where I can’t "hack" my way out of vulnerability anymore. It’s a hard lesson, but it’s the only one I have.

I’ve been thinking about the word "resilience." It’s a word everyone throws at me now—like, "You’re so resilient, Clarinda, look at your beautiful baby." And I am resilient, sure, but I hate that the word is used as a justification for the system’s failures. Like, because I managed to survive and come out the other side with a healthy child, it retroactively makes the dehumanization okay. It doesn't. 

I’m sitting here in Burlington, watching the sun hit the nursery floor, and I feel this phantom pressure in my chest—that same tightening I felt when the blood pressure cuff would constrict my arm. It’s a sensory memory of powerlessness. I wasn’t just a patient; I was a person being dismantled by a protocol that didn't have space for my voice.

I used to think that "clinical care" was the opposite of "humanity." I thought you had to choose between being safe and being heard. I’m starting to realize that the most dangerous thing about my experience wasn't just the eclampsia itself—it was the isolation that came with the treatment. If they had just *talked* to me, if they had acknowledged the person behind the blood-pressure spikes, maybe I wouldn't feel like I’m still mourning the version of myself that existed before I walked into that delivery room.

I want to know that my daughter’s first breath wasn't just a clinical success, but a moment that was held with care. I’m trying to make peace with the fact that I couldn't control the outcome, but it’s harder to make peace with the way I was treated while we were fighting for that life. I’m done being a "low-risk" box to be checked. I’m just a woman, learning to trust her own skin again. That’s all. Just trying to breathe.
Were you given unsolicited comments about your weight, diet, or physical appearance? How did those comments land?

You know, they always masked it under the guise of "clinical assessment," but it was there—this subtle, persistent judgment that felt less like health advice and more like a critique of my discipline. Because I spent so much time tracking my own metrics, I was hyper-aware of every pound, every intake measurement. But when a provider would look at my chart—or worse, look at *me*—and make a comment about my diet or weight gain, it felt like a moral failing, not a biological one.

There was one appointment where the provider basically lectured me on "responsible nutrition" because my weight gain was slightly outside the "optimal" range they had printed on their little chart. I was exhausted, I was dealing with the underlying dread of a pregnancy that felt precarious, and hearing that felt like a slap. It was the furthest thing from supportive; it was condescending.

It lands like a weight—ironically. It makes you feel like your body is a project you’re failing at. Every time they brought it up, I felt smaller, more defensive, and even more determined to just get through the appointment without being "the difficult one." They didn't see the woman navigating a complex reality; they saw a set of inputs they could critique. It’s hard enough to trust your body when it’s going through a trauma like eclampsia; being told it’s "wrong" on top of that? It’s just another layer of isolation.

It is all connected, isn't it? The way they talk about your body, the way they dismiss your intuition, the way they treat your labor like a process to be optimized—it all sends the same message: *we* are in charge of this, not you. 

Even now, months later, I find myself second-guessing my own physical sensations. When I’m tired, I wonder if it’s "just me" or if there’s a real, physiological reason for it that I should be reporting. I’ve lost that internal compass I spent years calibrating. The system didn’t just treat the eclampsia; it left me feeling like a stranger in my own physiology.

When I look at my daughter, I don’t just see the miracle of her birth—I see the miracle of our survival *despite* the way we were handled. I feel like I’m finally reclaiming the space between my own skin and the world. It’s not a grand, dramatic recovery. It’s just me, in my kitchen, learning to trust my heartbeat again without wondering if a monitor needs to verify it first. 

I’m sharing all of this because I hope that somewhere, in some clinic in Burlington or beyond, a provider might listen to the woman in the chair and realize that the most potent medical tool they have isn't the blood pressure cuff—it’s their own humanity. It’s just listening. Truly listening. That’s all.
Which informal sources — people or communities — did you turn to first, before or instead of a healthcare provider?

I went to my old, worn-out anatomy textbooks first, honestly. It’s almost laughable now, but I went back to the source—the dry, technical, clinical diagrams—trying to find some explanation for why I felt so wrong. I was essentially playing medical researcher on my own body because I felt like I couldn't trust the feedback loop from the actual clinic. I wanted to see if I could "read" the symptoms myself before having to endure another dismissive phone call with a triage nurse.

I also reached out to an old friend from my college days who works in public health—someone who understands the *systemic* side of things, not just the clinical. She didn't try to "fix" me, and she didn't give me advice I didn't ask for. She just listened, and she told me I wasn't crazy for feeling the way I did. That was, and is, my most precious resource. It wasn't about her giving me a diagnosis; it was about her validating the fact that the way I was being treated was, in her words, "a failure of the structure."

That validation—that I wasn't just an "anxious patient"—was the only thing that kept me from fully imploding. I wish that kind of validation had come from my doctor. It would have saved me so much heartache.

I’ve been thinking a lot about the word "validation." It’s become this strange, quiet obsession. When you’re in the middle of a medical event like eclampsia, you are so thirsty for someone—*anyone*—to say, "Yes, your body is doing this, and it is terrifying, and you are doing a heroic job just breathing through it."

Instead, the medical system is set up to strip away that validation. They want you to be a calm, quiet, data-compliant entity. When you ask for that human recognition, they act like you’re disrupting the clinical flow. They don’t see that *that* recognition is actually part of the healing process.

Now, as I look back, I’m trying to stop being the researcher of my own trauma and start being the person who lived it. I’m starting to trust my gut again. If I feel something is wrong, I don't look for a medical journal to prove I’m right—I just act on it. I’m learning that my intuition isn't a symptom of "high-risk" pregnancy, but a vital piece of the human experience that was ignored for far too long.

I’m finally realizing that the most important thing I can teach my daughter isn’t the anatomy of her own body, but the fact that her voice—her feelings, her boundaries, her sense of self—is the most accurate diagnostic tool she will ever possess. That’s the legacy I want to build. Not one of clinical control, but of radical, unapologetic self-trust. It’s hard work, but I think I’m finally starting to get the hang of it.
Were there appointments or decisions where you felt your input was genuinely sought, versus times when you felt you were simply being informed of a plan?

I think the answer to that is a hard "informed." There were almost no moments where I felt like my input was being genuinely sought, not in the way that matters. The communication was always top-down. 

Even in the "routine" checkups before the eclampsia, the questions they asked were closed-ended: "Any swelling? Any headaches?"—they weren't asking for my *perspective*. They weren't asking, "How are you feeling in your body today?" or "What’s been the most difficult part of this week for you?" They were mining me for data points to check against a protocol. 

When things became high-risk, it was even worse. It was pure theater. They would "inform" me of a plan that had already been decided in the hallway. They’d say, "We’re going to do X, Y, and Z to stabilize," and by the time I could even mentally process the list, they were already moving. If I tried to interject, it was like hitting a brick wall. My concerns were treated as irrational, as if my fear was a symptom of the condition itself that needed to be managed, not a valid expression of my own experience. 

It’s like being told you’re the captain of a ship, but the steering wheel is just a plastic toy while someone else is actually controlling the engine from behind a curtain. I wasn't a partner; I was a passenger who was kept in the dark about the destination.

I feel like I’ve spent months trying to explain this, and I still feel like I’m hitting that same glass wall. The frustration isn't just that I was silenced—it's that I was given the *illusion* of a seat at the table. They’d ask, "Do you have any questions?" right as they were walking out the door, their hand already on the handle. That's not a conversation. That’s a polite way of saying, "You don't get to speak."

If I could go back to that first trimester, I’d tell myself to stop apologizing for existing in that space. I’d tell myself that being "compliant" doesn't buy you safety—it only buys you invisibility. I spent so much energy trying to be the "good" patient so they would see me, and all it did was make me easier to ignore.

My daughter is sleeping now, and for the first time in a long time, the house is quiet. Not the "hospital quiet" that feels like it’s holding its breath, but a real, peaceful silence. I’m starting to realize that the power I was looking for—that ability to control my body's trajectory—doesn't come from a medical degree or a perfect birth plan. It comes from trusting the person I am, even when I'm scared. I survived the eclampsia, and I'm surviving this aftermath. I’m not just a data point anymore. I’m just me. And that has to be enough.
Was there a moment when your trust in a provider was broken — something they said, missed, or failed to explain?

It wasn’t one grand betrayal, but a slow erosion. There was this moment when I finally gathered the courage to say, "I feel like something is fundamentally wrong with how my body is processing the load—it’s not just normal third-trimester fatigue." I brought the symptoms, the timeline, the correlation I’d noted. And the provider just looked at their tablet, gave a thin, pitying smile, and said, "It’s a very normal pregnancy, Clarinda. You’re over-analyzing."

That was the moment the door slammed shut. She didn’t just ignore the data; she invalidated my very perception of my own existence. When that pressure in my head started—the one that eventually signaled the eclampsia—I didn't call the clinic for a long time. Why would I? I had been told by an "expert" that I was just an over-analyzing woman. That failure to listen, that dismissive labeling of my intuition as "anxiety," is exactly what led me to delay seeking help until I was practically at death's door.

When the crisis actually hit and the same team who told me I was "fine" started rushing around me, I felt a deep, sickening sense of vertigo. How could they be so casual about my health one day and so frantic the next? They never acknowledged that they missed it. They never apologized. They just moved on to the next set of protocols, as if their initial dismissal hadn't been the catalyst for the emergency. My trust wasn't broken; it was shredded, and it’s still in pieces.

I think that’s the hardest part to articulate—the sheer, cold lack of accountability in the room once the crisis actually began. It’s like they were operating in a vacuum where the past didn’t exist, as if their initial dismissal of my concerns was just a neutral mistake rather than a catastrophic oversight. They were too busy "saving" me to ever acknowledge that they were also the ones who had put me in that position in the first place by not listening when I tried to warn them.

It’s a strange, bitter pill to swallow—knowing that my own body was screaming for help, and I had been convinced by the people who were supposed to be its caretakers that I was just being dramatic. Every time I hear someone talk about "patient-centered care" now, I feel like laughing, or maybe crying. Because if I was "centered" in their care, I would have been heard weeks before that seizure.

I’m moving on, but the scar is there. Every time I have to engage with a medical professional now, I’m not just the patient; I’m the survivor of a system that didn't know how to see me. I’m starting to trust my own instincts again, but I’ve realized that I don't need their permission to be right about my own life. That’s the power I’m reclaiming—the right to know my body better than anyone else ever will.
Were there appointments, scans, or care moments where your partner's absence left you feeling isolated or unsupported?

I’m a single woman, and that detail of my life always seemed to put the staff on edge. Being on my own during this—without a partner to act as a buffer or a second set of ears—made the isolation feel absolute. 

There were so many moments, especially when the eclampsia diagnosis hit, where I felt like I was drowning because there was no one in my corner who was *actually* allowed to be there. I’d look around the room at these couples—partners holding hands, asking the questions I was too terrified to voice—and I felt like I was playing a game with the difficulty setting turned all the way up. 

When the doctors would talk to me, they’d often use this condescending "you’re doing this alone" tone, or they’d ask, "Is there anyone here who can help you make these decisions?" It felt like they were viewing my single status as a medical liability. It made me feel like I had to perform twice as well just to be taken seriously. I wasn't just managing a crisis; I was managing the social stigma of navigating that crisis solo. 

I don't think they realized how much their pity—or their impatience—added to the weight I was already carrying. I needed an advocate, but the system isn't built to support the woman who arrives alone. It’s built for pairs. Being on my own just made the silence of the room feel that much louder.

It wasn’t just the logistics of being alone, either. It was the way they spoke *at* me—like I was a problem to be solved because I didn't have a partner to help me "manage" the pregnancy. There were times when the doctors would ask, "Who is at home to monitor your blood pressure?" or "Who is helping you with the household duties?" as if they were questioning my capacity to handle the requirements of being a high-risk patient.

I felt like I was being judged for the very life circumstances I was living. I remember one conversation in particular where I was trying to explain that I was working full-time and managing this on my own, and the nurse just looked at me with a mix of pity and frustration, as if I had failed the system by not having a built-in support person. It made me feel like I was already one step behind, that my single status was somehow contributing to the "clinical instability" they were obsessed with.

I had to be my own advocate, my own caregiver, my own researcher, and my own emotional anchor. And when I finally collapsed, I felt like they blamed me for not having a partner to catch me. That sense of failure—that my solitary life was somehow "lesser" or "insufficient" in their eyes—is a wound that’s been slow to heal. I’m proud of what I did, but I’m still angry that I had to do it all while being treated like I was the one who was inadequate.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the one thing that still sits with me—the thing I keep coming back to—is the idea that we, as mothers, are expected to just "forget" the trauma because the end result is a baby. It's like we’re supposed to trade our autonomy for a birth certificate and call it a fair bargain. But it isn't. The way we are treated matters just as much as the way our babies are delivered.

I want the system to understand that when a woman walks through those doors, she brings her whole life with her. She brings her fears, her intelligence, her history of loss, and her own agency. When you strip that away and treat her like a biological event, you’re not just providing "clinical care"—you’re breaking something that may never fully mend.

I hope that by talking about this, maybe it nudges someone—a resident, a nurse, an administrator—to pause for an extra ten seconds before they issue an order. To look at the woman in the bed and see a person who is terrified, who is intelligent, and who is doing the hardest thing a human being can do. My pregnancy didn't have to be a battle for my own humanity. I hope the next woman who walks into that room in Burlington gets a partner, not a warden. That’s all I really want. Thank you for listening to the parts of this story that don't fit into the medical charts. It means more than I can say.
